response:
# Simple Button Navigation Accessibility Design Plan > **For Claude:** Use a11y-planner protocol. Review with a11y-critic after implementation. > **Compliance target:** WCAG 2.2 AA > **Users who need accessibility:** Keyboard-only users, NVDA screen reader users, low vision users > **Assistive technologies:** NVDA, keyboard-only navigation **Feature:** A reusable horizontal button bar with Save, Cancel, and Delete actions, each shown with icon plus text. **Risk Level:** Low **Component/Page Type:** Button group / action bar --- ## Scope & Context This component is a simple static action bar with three independent action buttons: Save, Cancel, and Delete. It does not need a toolbar, menu, roving tabindex, focus trap, live region, or custom keyboard model. The main accessibility risk is over-engineering: using `div`, custom roles, icon-only names, or JavaScript key handling when native buttons already provide the correct semantics and keyboard behavior. This plan is not claiming the actions themselves are safe, reversible, or confirmed. If Delete is destructive, the broader flow may need a confirmation dialog or undo pattern, but that is outside this button bar’s stated scope. ## Semantic Structure Plan Use a semantic grouping container only if the button bar needs a programmatic group label. For a reusable action bar, use either: ```html
[Save button] [Cancel button] [Delete button]
``` or a plain container if nearby heading/context already names the actions. Each control must be a native HTML ` ``` Do not give decorative icons their own alt text or accessible name, because that can cause redundant announcements like “disk Save button.” If an SVG icon is used, mark it `aria-hidden="true"` and prevent it from receiving focus. If the final design becomes icon-only at any breakpoint, each button must receive an explicit accessible name: ```html ``` WCAG grounding: - Decorative icons hidden from assistive tech: WCAG 1.1.1 Non-text Content. - Button accessible name must be present: WCAG 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value. - Visible text labels are preferred for cognitive and recognition support: WCAG 2.4.6 Headings and Labels. ## Focus Management Plan Tab order should follow DOM order and visual order, left to right: 1. Save 2. Cancel 3. Delete No roving tabindex. No arrow key navigation. No focus trap. No focus restoration requirement inside this component. Each button must remain reachable by Tab and show a visible focus indicator. Use `:focus-visible` styling so keyboard users get a strong indicator without adding unnecessary mouse-click rings. Focus indicator requirement: - Minimum 3:1 contrast against adjacent colors. - At least a 2px outline or equivalent visible perimeter. - Prefer a two-color focus ring, such as a dark outline plus light offset ring, so it remains visible on different button backgrounds. WCAG grounding: - Logical focus order: WCAG 2.4.3 Focus Order. - Visible focus indicator: WCAG 2.4.7 Focus Visible. - Focus appearance and contrast: WCAG 2.4.11 Focus Not Obscured, WCAG 2.4.13 Focus Appearance, WCAG 1.4.11 Non-text Contrast. ## State Communication Design | State | Visual Indicator | Programmatic Indicator | ARIA Attribute | WCAG Citation | |---|---|---|---|---| | Default | Normal button styling | Native button role/name | None needed | 4.1.2 | | Hover | Background/border change | Not required | None | 1.4.11 | | Active/pressed moment | Brief pressed visual style | Native button activation | Do not use `aria-pressed` unless it is a toggle | 4.1.2 | | Focused | 2px+ visible focus ring, 3:1 contrast | Browser focus state | None needed | 2.4.7, 2.4.13 | | Disabled, if supported later | Reduced opacity plus text/icon styling | Native `disabled` attribute | Prefer `disabled`, not `aria-disabled`, for native buttons | 4.1.2 | | Danger action | Red/destructive styling plus text label “Delete” and destructive icon | Button name includes “Delete” | None required | 1.4.1, 4.1.2 | Do not use `aria-pressed` for Save, Cancel, or Delete unless one of them becomes a persistent toggle button. These are momentary action buttons, not selected states. ## Visual Accessibility Plan Normal and danger buttons must be visually distinct by more than color alone. Plan: - Save: primary or normal action styling with save icon and “Save” text. - Cancel: secondary/neutral styling with cancel icon and “Cancel” text. - Delete: danger styling with delete/trash icon and “Delete” text. - Danger state must not rely only on red. The label “Delete” and icon provide the non-color alternative. Contrast requirements: - Button text: 4.5:1 minimum against button background, WCAG 1.4.3. - Icon and border: 3:1 minimum against adjacent background, WCAG 1.4.11. - Focus ring: 3:1 minimum contrast against adjacent colors, WCAG 2.4.13. - Target size: at least 24x24 CSS px minimum, 44x44 recommended, WCAG 2.5.8. Hover and active states must preserve text and icon contrast. At 200% zoom, labels must remain visible and not overlap. ## Content Accessibility Plan Button labels should be short, visible, and action-specific: - “Save” - “Cancel” - “Delete” Avoid vague labels like “OK” or icon-only labels unless the icon-only version has `aria-label`. If Delete is destructive in the broader product flow, consider confirmation or undo elsewhere. This plan only covers the button bar itself. ## Testing Strategy ### Test Cases - Keyboard navigation: Tab reaches Save, then Cancel, then Delete in left-to-right order. - Keyboard activation: Enter activates each button. - Keyboard activation: Space activates each button. - Focus visibility: each focused button shows a visible indicator with at least 3:1 contrast. - Screen reader: NVDA announces each as “Save button,” “Cancel button,” and “Delete button.” - Icon accessibility: decorative icons are not announced separately. - DOM verification: each control is a native `button`, not a `div` with click handlers. - DOM verification: if icon-only rendering exists at any breakpoint, each button has an accessible name via `aria-label`. - Visual distinction: Delete is distinguishable from normal buttons by label, icon, and styling, not color alone. - Contrast: text, icons, borders, and focus indicators meet required contrast in default, hover, active, and focus states. ## Implementation Tasks ### Task 1: Button Bar Semantics **Structure Stub:** ```html
``` **ARIA Attributes:** - `role="group"`: only if the group needs a programmatic label; supports WCAG 1.3.1. - `aria-label="Actions"`: names the button group; supports WCAG 4.1.2. - `aria-hidden="true"` on decorative icons: prevents redundant icon announcements; supports WCAG 1.1.1. - `aria-label` on button: only if visible text is removed; supports WCAG 4.1.2. **Keyboard Interactions:** - Tab: moves through Save, Cancel, Delete. - Enter: activates focused button. - Space: activates focused button. - Arrow keys: no custom behavior. **WCAG Criteria:** - WCAG 2.1.1 Keyboard - WCAG 2.4.3 Focus Order - WCAG 2.4.7 Focus Visible - WCAG 2.4.13 Focus Appearance - WCAG 1.1.1 Non-text Content - WCAG 1.4.1 Use of Color - WCAG 1.4.3 Contrast Minimum - WCAG 1.4.11 Non-text Contrast - WCAG 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value ## a11y-Critic Review Checkpoint | Checkpoint | After Task | Focus Areas | |---|---|---| | 1 | Button bar implementation | Verify native buttons, accessible names, icon hiding, keyboard activation, tab order, visible focus, contrast, and danger styling non-color alternative | ## References - WAI-ARIA APG Button Pattern: https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/button/ - WCAG 2.2 Quick Reference: https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/quickref/
